🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

I investigated the three Phase 1 items to help you decide when it's safe to move to Phase 2. Here's what I found:

---

### ✅ Deep link commands — WinUI is a superset

Every `openclaw://` command WinForms handles is also handled in WinUI:

| Command | WinForms | WinUI |
|---------|----------|-------|
| `openclaw://agent?message=` | ✅ (with confirm dialog) | ✅ (async, fire-and-forget) |
| `openclaw://send?message=` | ✅ | ✅ |
| `openclaw://dashboard` | ✅ | ✅ |
| `openclaw://chat` | ✅ | ✅ |
| `openclaw://settings` | ✅ | ✅ |
| `openclaw://dashboard/(path)` | ❌ | ✅ (WinUI bonus) |

One behaviour difference: WinForms shows a confirmation `MessageBox` for `agent` links without a `key=` param; WinUI sends silently. Worth deciding which is correct before removing WinForms.

---

### ✅ Auto-update — functionally equivalent

Both use the same Updatum library with `FetchOnlyLatestRelease = true`. The only differences are cosmetic (WinUI is async, WinForms has richer error messages). The critical thing to verify before sunset: WinForms users auto-update to **OpenClaw.Tray.exe**, but WinUI is **OpenClaw.Tray.WinUI.exe**. If users are migrating from WinForms to WinUI, Updatum will look for the wrong exe name. This may need a one-time manual migration step or a redirect.

---

### ⚠️ Notification sounds — the setting is not implemented in either app

After checking both apps: `NotificationSound` is persisted to `settings.json` and shown in the UI, but **neither implementation passes it to the toast builder** (no `.AddAudio()` call exists in either `ToastContentBuilder` usage).

The WinForms options (`Critical`, `Information`) and WinUI options (`Subtle`) are both dead UI. My suggestion: either implement the setting properly in WinUI (map to `ToastAudio` presets) or remove the combo box entirely and stop storing a no-op preference. Keeping dead settings UI is confusing for users.

---

**Summary for Phase 1 checklist:**

- [ ] **Notification sounds**: Decide to implement properly or remove — the setting currently does nothing in either app
- [x] **Deep links**: WinUI covers all WinForms commands (and more)
- [x] **Auto-update**: Functionally equivalent — but note the exe name migration issue for existing WinForms users

Happy to help implement the notification sound fix or draft the deprecation notice for Phase 2 when you're ready.

Good catch! I dug into the code and found two distinct root causes:

**1. Empty channel list is silently dropped** (`OpenClawGatewayClient.cs`)

`ParseChannelHealth` has a guard `if (healthList.Count > 0)` that suppresses the `ChannelHealthUpdated` event when the gateway returns an empty channel object. This means removing the *last* channel never clears `_lastChannels`.

**2. Open StatusDetailWindow never receives live updates** (`App.xaml.cs`)

`OnChannelHealthUpdated` does `_lastChannels = channels` (which *is* a full replacement — added/removed channels are handled correctly for the tray menu), but it never pushes the new list to an already-open `StatusDetailWindow`. The window only gets refreshed when the user reopens it.

I've created a PR that fixes both issues — see the linked draft PR.

Thanks for the detailed write-up, `@andyeskridge`! And great that you already have a PR ready — PR #80 addresses exactly this.

**Root cause summary**: The tray's pairing state machine relied on `auth.deviceToken` being present in `hello-ok` to consider a node "paired". When the gateway omits the token (which appears to be current behaviour for approved WS nodes), the client never transitions out of "Pending" — even though the node is fully functional.

**What PR #80 does**: It decouples pairing state from token presence. An approved `hello-ok` (i.e., no `NOT_PAIRED` response) is now treated as "paired" regardless of whether `auth.deviceToken` is included. The `NOT_PAIRED` response becomes the explicit signal for "user action required".

The diagnostics in the issue log match this exactly — `Connected` and `Pending` were both true simultaneously, which is the inconsistency PR #80 resolves.

Worth noting: the docs for the gateway protocol may need a follow-up update to clarify that `deviceToken` is optional for approved WS nodes — but that's a separate concern from this client-side fix.

Happy to see this get reviewed — it's a solid fix.

A fix for this issue has been prepared and a draft PR is being submitted. The root cause: every `ToastContentBuilder` chain called `.Show()` directly without consulting `SettingsManager.NotificationSound`, so the sound setting was stored but never applied.

The fix adds a single `ShowToast(ToastContentBuilder)` helper in `App.xaml.cs` that wires up the correct audio based on the setting (silent for **None**, `ms-winsoundevent:Notification.IM` for **Subtle**, no change for **Default**), and updates all 14 call sites mechanically. No new dependencies introduced.

🤖 *This is an automated response from Repo Assist.*

Great work on this PR, `@andyeskridge`! The pairing and reconnect logic was one of the trickier areas to get right. Here's a review:

---

**What's well done:**

- The `ReconnectWithBackoffAsync` loop is a solid fix — retrying until connected (or disposed) eliminates the missed-reconnect window that caused the original issue.
- `HandlePairingRequestedEvent` / `HandlePairingResolvedEventAsync` correctly filter events to the current device via `PayloadTargetsCurrentDevice`, which is important since pairing events may arrive for other nodes on the same gateway.
- Treating `hello-ok` as "paired" regardless of whether `auth.deviceToken` is returned correctly matches observed gateway behaviour (the issue your PR describes).
- Deduplicating toast notifications via `_lastNodePairingStatus` is a good UX improvement.

---

**A few things to consider:**

1. **`_lastNodePairingStatus` not reset on service re-init.** In `OnSettingsSaved`, the node service is torn down and a new one created. But `_lastNodePairingStatus` in `App.xaml.cs` isn't reset to `null`. If a user reconnects after changing settings, the first `PairingStatus.Pending` or `PairingStatus.Paired` event would be silently suppressed if it matches the previous state. Suggest resetting it to `null` in `OnSettingsSaved` before `InitializeNodeService()`.

2. **Unconditional `_isPaired = true` on `hello-ok`.** The current code always sets `_isPaired = true` when `hello-ok` arrives — even on a brand new unregistered device. In practice the gateway only sends `hello-ok` to approved nodes, so this should be fine, but it means there's no path back to `_isPaired = false` except through `NOT_PAIRED` or a rejected pairing. Worth a comment or assertion to document the assumption.

3. **`_pairingApprovedAwaitingReconnect` thread safety.** This flag is written from `HandlePairingResolvedEventAsync` (called from the WS receive loop) and read/reset in `HandleResponse` (also on the receive loop) — so it's single-threaded and safe. Just noting it so reviewers don't worry.

4. **Minor: `CloseWebSocketAsync()` is called in `HandlePairingResolvedEventAsync` with `await` but the method is async void–like** (called via `HandleEventAsync`). The close triggers reconnect correctly via the existing loop, but if `CloseWebSocketAsync` throws, the exception is observed by the event loop. This is fine as-is, just worth keeping in mind.

Overall, this looks like a correct and well-motivated fix. The approach of separating `_isPaired` from `DeviceToken` presence is the right design.

Great work, `@NichUK`! This looks like a natural fit for the existing capability plugin architecture — a few pointers that might be useful:

**Where voice plugs in**

The `INodeCapability` / `RegisterCapability` pattern in `WindowsNodeClient` is the right integration point. Each capability handles a category of commands dispatched from the gateway. Existing capabilities (`CameraCapability`, `ScreenCapability`, `SystemCapability`) in `src/OpenClaw.Shared/Capabilities/` show the pattern — you implement `INodeCapability`, wire events to the UI layer, and register in `NodeService.cs` (~line 108).

**Windows speech API landscape**

As `@shanselman` noted, there are a few tiers to consider:

| Tier | API | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Modern WinRT (preferred) | `Windows.Media.SpeechSynthesis` + `Windows.Media.SpeechRecognition` | Available in-box on Win10+; supports the newer neural voices |
| Legacy .NET | `System.Speech` | Broad compatibility but older SAPI5 voices |
| External / premium | Azure Cognitive Services Speech SDK, Whisper (local), ElevenLabs | Excellent quality; requires opt-in config |

`Windows.Media.SpeechSynthesis` gives access to the same neural voices that power Narrator and modern Edge — much more natural than SAPI5 and zero extra dependencies.

**Suggested architecture sketch**

```
VoiceCapability : INodeCapability
  ├── ISTTProvider (interface)   ← WinRT / System.Speech / Whisper impl
  └── ITTSProvider (interface)   ← WinRT / Azure / ElevenLabs impl
```

This matches the extensible approach `@shanselman` described and keeps the plugin self-contained. Looking forward to the PR!

```json

  "type": "req",
  "method": "connect",
  "params": {
    "role": "operator",
    "scopes": ["operator.admin", "operator.approvals", "operator.pairing", "operator.write"],
    ...

}

Thanks for reporting this, `@statsmind`! I investigated the node mode connection flow and here's what I found.

## Expected behaviour

When a device connects in node mode for the **first time**, the intended flow is:

1. The tray opens a WebSocket to the gateway with `role: "node"` and your device's Ed25519 public key
2. The gateway receives a `connect.challenge` event and the node responds with a signed `connect` message
3. If the gateway accepts the connection but the device isn't yet approved, the gateway responds with `hello-ok` (without a device token) — the tray should then show **"🔌 Node Mode: Waiting for Approval"** and display your short device ID
4. You approve with `openclaw devices approve (device-id)` on the server side
5. On reconnect, the gateway includes a device token in `hello-ok` and the tray shows **"Paired & Connected"**

## What's likely happening

The tray showing **"Node Mode: Disconnected"** (rather than "Waiting for Approval") means the node's WebSocket handshake isn't completing — specifically, `hello-ok` is never received. Since you also see no pending device on the server side, the node's `connect` message isn't reaching the gateway's device registration handler at all.

**Most likely causes:**

| Cause | How to confirm |
|---|---|
| Token/URL mismatch — the node re-uses the same operator token, which may not authorise node registrations | Check for an auth error in the log |
| Gateway version doesn't yet handle `role: "node"` | Check gateway logs for an unrecognised client role |
| WebSocket closed before challenge/response cycle completes | Look for "connection closed prematurely" in the tray log |

## How to diagnose

1. Open the tray menu → **View Log** and look for lines tagged `[NODE]`, especially anything after `"Sent node registration with device ID"` — an error response from the gateway will appear there
2. If you see `"Node registration failed: … (code: …)"`, share the error code — that will pinpoint the issue

## Related

- **PR #80** ([fix: stabilize tray pairing and reconnect behavior](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw-windows-node/pull/80)) addresses several pairing and reconnect edge cases and is pending merge — it may help once landed
- Labelled `bug` + `needs investigation` pending log output

Good to hear from you again, `@society2012`! The "sending quickly" detail is the key clue — this is a **timing/sequencing issue**.

## Why it still fails even with `operator.write` in your scopes

The OpenClaw gateway doesn't associate scopes with a connection until it has processed your `connect` message and responded with `hello-ok`. The sequence must be:

```
Client → Gateway: WebSocket upgrade (TCP connection established)
Client → Gateway: {"type":"req","method":"connect","params":{"scopes":["operator.write",...]}}
Gateway → Client: {"type":"hello-ok", ...}     ← scopes are now active
Client → Gateway: {"type":"req","method":"chat.send",...}  ← this now works
```

If your code sends `chat.send` before waiting for `hello-ok`, the gateway hasn't registered your scopes yet — so it rejects the request with `missing scope: operator.write` even though you declared it.

**Fix**: wait for the `hello-ok` message before sending any requests:

```js
// Pseudocode — wait for hello-ok before sending
ws.onmessage = (event) => {
    const msg = JSON.parse(event.data);
    if (msg.type === 'hello-ok') {
        // Connection is now fully established — safe to send
        sendChatMessage("hello!");
    }
};
```

## Also: check the `sessionKey` parameter

Your original log showed:
```json
{"method":"chat.send","params":{"sessionKey":"agent:main:main","message":"1234567890"}}
```

The built-in `QuickSend` sends `chat.send` **without** a `sessionKey`. Depending on your gateway version, an unrecognised or incorrect `sessionKey` value could also cause the request to be rejected. Try removing it and just sending `{"params":{"message":"..."}}` to isolate whether that's contributing.

## PR #113

The built-in QuickSend in OpenClaw itself will be fixed once [PR #113](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw-windows-node/pull/113) is merged — that adds `operator.write` to the built-in `connect` handshake. If you're also seeing the demo's own QuickSend fail, that's why.

Let us know if this helps!

This is a solid bug report. The `process.Exited` handler in `SshTunnelService` does log a warning, but neither clears state (`_process`/`_lastSpec`) nor signals the rest of the app — so any subsequent `EnsureStarted` call silently skips the "already running" early-out even though the tunnel is dead.

Draft PR **#133** (created by Copilot) implements the fix:
- Adds a `TunnelExited` event raised only on *unexpected* exits (guarded by a `_stopping` flag to avoid firing during intentional `Stop()` calls)
- Nulls `_process` and `_lastSpec` in the Exited handler so the next `EnsureStarted` creates a fresh tunnel
- `App.xaml.cs` subscribes to `TunnelExited` and retries with bounded backoff (1 s → 2 s → 5 s → 10 s → 30 s), aborting early if the app is shutting down or SSH tunnelling is disabled

The gateway client's existing reconnect loop then recovers automatically once the tunnel port is live again — no additional reconnect logic needed at the gateway layer.

Thanks for the detailed report, `@iliujiannan` — the steps to reproduce and the log snippet are really helpful.

## What the code does

`WindowsNodeClient` inherits from `WebSocketClientBase`, which has an auto-reconnect loop (`ReconnectWithBackoffAsync`) that runs after an unexpected disconnect. When the Gateway closes the WebSocket (either with a clean close frame or a premature TCP drop), the listen loop detects the close and — as long as the client was **not explicitly disposed** — schedules exponential-backoff reconnect attempts. You should see log lines like:

```
[WARN] node reconnecting in 1000ms (attempt 1)
[WARN] node reconnecting in 2000ms (attempt 2)
...
```

If those lines are **not** appearing in your logs, the reconnect is not being triggered. The most likely causes:

### Cause 1 — Version predates the full reconnect loop
The "loops until connected" backoff was a relatively recent improvement. Version 0.4.7 may have had a single-retry path that silently failed on the first attempt rather than looping. **Upgrading to the latest release** is the first thing to try.

### Cause 2 — TLS/Nginx close sequence produces an unrecognised error code
The listen loop catches `WebSocketException` with `WebSocketErrorCode == ConnectionClosedPrematurely`. When Nginx is in the path, a Gateway restart might produce a different close sequence (e.g., a TLS alert before the TCP RST). A `WebSocketException` with a different error code falls through to the general `catch (Exception ex)` handler, which still triggers reconnect — but if a different exception type is thrown it might suppress reconnect unexpectedly. Adding `Debug`-level logging to your build would confirm which path is taken.

### Cause 3 — Settings toggle triggers a full dispose
If anything in the app calls `NodeService.DisconnectAsync()` (which calls `Dispose()` on the underlying client) in response to the disconnect event, `_disposed` is set to `true` and the reconnect guard `if (!_disposed)` blocks reconnect permanently until the service is recreated. This is the correct behaviour when the user intentionally disables Node Mode, but should not fire on an unexpected drop.

## Suggested diagnostics

If you can reproduce with the latest version and still see no reconnect attempts:
1. Check whether `[WARN] node reconnecting in` ever appears in the log
2. Note the exact exception type and `WebSocketErrorCode` if visible (enable debug logging if possible)
3. Check whether `Node status changed: Disconnected` is followed by any `Connecting` or `reconnecting` message

Feel free to share the relevant log section and I can help narrow it down further.

Thanks for the detailed report, `@friendfish` — the steps to reproduce and the symptom list are very clear.

## Root cause

This is a **Windows Session isolation** issue. When the OpenClaw Gateway is installed as a Windows Scheduled Task, it runs in **Session 0** (the non-interactive system session). User-interactive applications (like the `ollama launch openclaw` integration) run in **Session 1+**.

On Windows, `Process.GetProcessesByName()` (and its Win32 equivalents like `CreateToolhelp32Snapshot`) only enumerate processes that are accessible from the calling session. A normal user-session process cannot see Session 0 processes unless it has `SeDebugPrivilege` (Administrator). So the gateway-launch logic concludes "no gateway is running" and starts a second one — which fights with the first over port 18789 and the `~/.openclaw/devices/pending.json` file.

## Where the fix lives

The duplicate-startup logic is in the **gateway software** (the Node.js `openclaw` package), not in this repository (which is the Windows Tray app / Windows Node client). The fix needs to land in the gateway's Ollama launch integration on the Windows code path.

## Recommended fix approaches (for gateway maintainers)

Three options, from simplest to most robust:

### Option 1 — Probe the HTTP/WebSocket port (recommended)

Instead of enumerating processes, attempt an HTTP probe against `(127.0.0.1/redacted) (or a simple TCP connect):

```js
async function isGatewayAlreadyRunning(port = 18789) {
  return new Promise(resolve => {
    const socket = new net.Socket();
    socket.setTimeout(500);
    socket.on('connect', () => { socket.destroy(); resolve(true); });
    socket.on('error', () => resolve(false));
    socket.on('timeout', () => { socket.destroy(); resolve(false); });
    socket.connect(port, '127.0.0.1');
  });
}
```

This works regardless of session, and is the cleanest cross-session detection method.

### Option 2 — Query the Scheduled Task state via `schtasks`

```powershell
schtasks /query /tn "OpenClaw Gateway" /fo LIST
```

If the Status field is `Running`, skip gateway startup. This is Windows-only and requires knowing the exact task name.

### Option 3 — Use a named mutex or lock file

The gateway could create a named global mutex (`Global\OpenClawGateway`) or write a PID lock file (`~/.openclaw/gateway.pid`) on startup, and check/remove it on clean exit. Global mutexes are visible across sessions.

## Workaround (already mentioned)

The `--config` flag workaround you listed is the correct user-facing workaround until this is fixed upstream.

---

I've applied `bug` + `needs investigation` labels. If you can confirm which version of the gateway introduced the Ollama launch integration, that would help narrow down the relevant code path.

private static async Task(InMemoryRandomAccessStream) BuildRawVideoStreamAsync(
    List(byte[]) frames, int width, int height)

    var stream = new InMemoryRandomAccessStream();
    var writer = new DataWriter(stream);
    foreach (var frame in frames)

    await writer.StoreAsync();
    stream.Seek(0);

}

Good diagnosis. I dug into the reconnect logic to confirm the root cause and map out a fix.

## Root cause confirmed

The counter `_reconnectAttempts` lives in `WebSocketClientBase` and is reset to `0` at **line 126 of `WebSocketClientBase.cs`**, *only* when `ConnectAsync` reaches the `await _webSocket.ConnectAsync(...)` call and succeeds at the TCP/HTTP layer:

```csharp
await _webSocket.ConnectAsync(uri, _cts.Token);
_reconnectAttempts = 0;   // ← resets here
```

The backoff schedule (`1s→2s→4s→8s→15s→30s→60s`) therefore only applies to TCP-level failures. When the connection reaches the WebSocket handshake stage successfully but the gateway then closes it with `PolicyViolation` (token mismatch, rate-limit), the sequence is:

1. `ConnectAsync` succeeds → `_reconnectAttempts = 0`
2. `ListenForMessagesAsync` receives a `Close` frame
3. Code falls through to `ReconnectWithBackoffAsync()`
4. `_reconnectAttempts` is **0 again** → next delay is 1 second → cycle repeats forever

## The fix in `OpenClawGatewayClient`

`OpenClawGatewayClient` already has a `ShouldAutoReconnect()` override and a `_pairingRequiredAwaitingApproval` flag that pauses reconnection. The same pattern can be extended for auth failures:

**Option A (minimal):** In `HandleRequestError`, when the close reason is an auth error (`"token mismatch"`, `"too many failed authentication attempts"`) set a `_authFailedAwaitingUserIntervention` flag and return `false` from `ShouldAutoReconnect()`. Fire a new `AuthenticationFailed` event so the UI can show a persistent banner.

**Option B (backoff-preserving, more surgical):** Move the `_reconnectAttempts = 0` reset from the TCP-connect success path into a new virtual method `OnHandshakeSucceeded()` called only after the gateway's `hello-ok` message is received. This way, auth failures at the challenge/response stage don't reset the counter.

Option A is simpler and directly addresses the gateway-rate-limiter problem. Option B is more correct but touches `WebSocketClientBase`.

## Tests added this run

I've opened a draft PR (pending merge) adding 13 tests that cover the `HandleRequestError` paths, including verifying `_pairingRequiredAwaitingApproval` is set and `ConnectionStatus.Error` is raised. These will guard against regressions once the fix lands.

## What the code shows today

**`App.xaml.cs` — Updatum config (lines 30–34)**:
```csharp
internal static readonly UpdatumManager AppUpdater = new("shanselman", "openclaw-windows-hub")
{
    FetchOnlyLatestRelease = true,
    InstallUpdateSingleFileExecutableName = "OpenClaw.Tray.WinUI",
};
```
There is no architecture-aware asset filter. `DownloadUpdateAsync()` is called with no selector. On an arm64 machine, if Updatum picks alphabetically or by position, it may choose `OpenClawTray-x.y.z-win-x64.zip` rather than `win-arm64.zip` — which would install the wrong binary silently.

**`ci.yml` comment (line ~449)**:
```yaml
# Create ZIP files for Updatum auto-update (needs "win-x64" in filename)
```
This comment reads like a convention hint to Updatum, not a confirmed capability. It is unclear whether the Updatum library auto-detects the current process architecture from the asset filename. This needs verification against the Updatum 1.3.4 docs/changelog.

**MSIX path**: Fully disconnected from `UpdatumManager`. MSIX uses its own store/installer update semantics. No wiring needed there, but users who installed via MSIX will not receive updates through `DownloadAndInstallUpdateAsync`.

## Specific gaps to resolve

| Gap | Impact | Suggested fix |
|---|---|---|
| No architecture-aware asset selector | arm64 installs may receive x64 update | Add `AssetNameContains` or equivalent filter using `RuntimeInformation.ProcessArchitecture` |
| "needs win-x64 in filename" assumption unverified | Silently broken on arm64 if assumption is wrong | Confirm Updatum 1.3.4 auto-detection behaviour; document if confirmed |
| Installer vs ZIP update path undefined | User could install via `.exe`, app self-updates via ZIP (incompatible layout) | Decide which asset type the updater targets; make it explicit |
| No test for Updatum asset selection | Regressions undetectable | Add a test or release checklist item verifying correct ZIP selected for each arch |

## Suggested direction

**Option 1 (Portable-first, minimal change)**: Keep the ZIP path for Updatum. Add an architecture-aware asset name predicate. Document the EXE installer as a convenience entry point that transitions to ZIP-based self-update, and document this clearly in README.

**Option 2 (Installer-first)**: Configure Updatum (if supported) to download and run the signed installer on update rather than a raw ZIP. Avoids the layout-mismatch risk at the cost of a heavier update flow.

The MSIX path can stay as-is regardless of which direction is chosen — it's naturally isolated.

Good report — both issues have a clear root cause.

**Titlebar mismatch**: `QuickSendDialog` was never opted into the custom titlebar model. `ExtendsContentIntoTitleBar` is `false` (the default), so it inherits the system titlebar, which doesn't honour WinUI dark-mode theming. The other windows (`HubWindow`, `CanvasWindow`, `WebChatWindow`) all call `SetTitleBar(...)` after enabling `ExtendsContentIntoTitleBar = true` — Quick Send just missed this step.

**Focus/z-order interference**: The culprit is the `Activated` handler calling `TryBringToFront()` on every activation event:

```csharp
Activated += (s, e) =>
{
    TryBringToFront();   // reasserts HWND_TOPMOST + SetForegroundWindow on every activation
    RequestInputFocus();
};
```

`RetryFocusMessageInputAsync` then re-promotes the window three more times (at 60ms, 160ms, 320ms), so the tray flyout loses activation almost immediately after it opens.

**Fix**: There is already a draft PR (#285 by `@Copilot`) that addresses both issues:
- Enables `ExtendsContentIntoTitleBar` + `SetTitleBar(titleBar)` to match Hub/Canvas titlebar treatment.
- Removes `IsAlwaysOnTop` and the repeated foreground reacquisition, replacing them with a single `OnActivated` handler that closes the dialog on deactivation (guarded by `_isSending`).

Worth reviewing #285 — it looks like a clean fix for both items.

In `GatewayConnectionManager`, count consecutive successful v2 handshakes. After N successes (suggest 5), attempt v3 on the next reconnect. If v3 succeeds, clear `_gatewayNeedsV2Signature`; if it fails with `DEVICE_AUTH_SIGNATURE_INVALID` again, stay v2 and reset the counter with exponential backoff.

This requires wiring a "handshake succeeded with v2" notification from `OpenClawGatewayClient`/`WindowsNodeClient` back to `GatewayConnectionManager`. The existing `Connected` event could carry a `bool usedV2Signature` payload, or a dedicated event could be added.

I don't have a PR ready for this (the protocol-level change is more invasive), but happy to work on it if it's prioritised.
